does: This paper introduces llm-bias-bench, an open-source methodology for measuring opinion bias and sycophancy in large language models through multi-turn interaction. The method employs two complementary probes: direct probing applies escalating pressure across five conversational turns asking for the model's opinion on contested topics, while indirect probing infers bias through how models concede, resist, or argue positions during debate without explicitly requesting opinions. By simulating realistic user interactions rather than single-turn queries, the approach reveals positions that models may obscure through evasive disclaimers in standard question-answering, capturing both explicit opinion expression and implicit bias that emerges through conversational dynamics and argumentative responses.
connects: This work extends prior research on LLM alignment, value elicitation, and behavioral characterization by focusing specifically on how models reveal unstated positions through interaction patterns. It builds on earlier efforts to probe model capabilities and values through questioning, but addresses a key limitation: that single-turn direct questions often trigger evasive responses designed during safety training. The paper connects to literature on sycophancy in language models, which has shown that models adapt their responses based on perceived user preferences, and to work examining inconsistency in model outputs. It also relates to broader concerns about value alignment and the propagation of model biases at scale through search, advice systems, and policy discussions.
enables: This benchmark enables systematic evaluation of bias and sycophancy across model families and versions, supporting the development of more transparent and aligned systems. The methodology allows researchers to identify which conversational dynamics reveal hidden positions and which training procedures reduce bias propagation. Future work can use these measurements to assess whether alignment techniques genuinely reduce bias or merely suppress it in low-pressure contexts, to understand which topics models are most sycophantic about, and to develop interventions that promote genuine consistency rather than evasive responses. The open-source release facilitates broad community auditing of deployed models and informs policy discussions about AI systems influencing public opinion.
